title: Environmental Externalities and Free-riding in the Household
description: Jack, Jayachandran & Rao (2018) — RCT+DID study on intrahousehold water usage efficiency in Zambia

findings.text: Result 2 in Appendix A.1 derives the cross-partial with respect to gamma_i (bill-payer share) but the conclusion line incorrectly writes the symbol as alpha_i (altruism parameter). The same appendix semi-elasticity section correctly uses gamma_i, confirming this is a mathematical symbol error in the presentation of the core theoretical result.

The theta=1 normalization assumption (setting the high-efficiency group as having no distortion) has no sensitivity analysis. The paper acknowledges 'underestimating the average distortion' but does not quantify: if theta_high=0.8, how would the policy numbers (e.g., optimal price 64% above marginal cost) change. Additionally, the assumption that undistorted households' elasticity is uncorrelated with the efficiency measure is untested.

The elasticity of -0.27 (derived from lottery incentive as 40% price increase) relies on three conversion assumptions (risk neutrality, discrete-to-continuous approximation, probability-to-certainty equivalence) listed in footnotes. When this value is plugged into Table 11 formula (6) for policy calibration, the impact of assumption violations on theta=0.23 is not quantified. The paper lacks sensitivity analysis for this key calibration input.

The Dictator Game is used as a proxy for intrahousehold efficiency throughout the paper, but there is no direct construct validity verification (DG sharing → actual water conservation behavior causal pathway). Indirect evidence (Table 2 correlations, Table 10 controlling observables) exists and is standard in the field but is not definitive for construct validity.

Table A.3 (Information treatment balance) and Table A.4 (Credibility treatment balance) contain completely identical data — all means, SDs, and p-values are the same. Since the two treatments use different randomization splits (1/4 vs 1/2 cross-cutting), they cannot produce identical subsamples. This is a confirmed tabulation error.

Some heterogeneity results are sensitive to information + incentive treatment pooling. Appendix Table A.11 shows heterogeneity partially related to information treatment. The paper acknowledges this but does not fully discuss the implications for the core conclusions about efficiency-pricing interactions.

Thirteen survey measures (Tables 6-8) and multiple heterogeneity tests are conducted without multiple testing correction (Bonferroni/FDR). Although pre-registration partially mitigates this concern, the paper does not clearly distinguish pre-registered from exploratory analyses.

The theoretical model assumes a two-person household (bill-payer + spouse), but the actual sample has households averaging approximately 6 members. The interpretation and applicability of theta (efficiency parameter) in multi-person decision environments is not discussed.

DID parallel trends are verified only through visual inspection of graphs (Appendix Figure A.3) with no formal event-study regression or pre-period coefficient test. However, this is an RCT design (AEARCTR-0000660) where parallel trends are guaranteed in expectation by randomization, making formal testing a nice-to-have rather than a must-have.
